questions.prompt: Why is running containers as non-root important?

choices.text: Containers share the host kernel; a container compromise as root makes container escape and host compromise dramatically easier. Non-root + dropped capabilities + read-only filesystem + seccomp restrict what an attacker can do post-compromise: can't write to FS, can't install tools, can't make privileged syscalls. The same vulnerability has vastly smaller blast radius. Production posture: USER directive in Dockerfile + `--cap-drop=ALL` + `--read-only` + minimal base image.

questions.explanation: Non-root + dropped caps + read-only FS reduces blast radius on compromise. Standard production hardening.

questions.prompt: An operator's installation guide says `docker run --privileged ...`. Should you use it?

choices.text: Be skeptical. `--privileged` removes most isolation: all capabilities, no AppArmor/SELinux, no seccomp, device access, can mount filesystems. Compromise inside a privileged container is effectively host compromise. Many tools include `--privileged` in install guides for convenience when they actually need specific capabilities (e.g., NET_ADMIN for network tools, SYS_PTRACE for debuggers). Check what the container actually needs and grant specific capabilities: `--cap-add=NET_ADMIN` instead of full privileged. If the docs don't explain why privileged is needed, push back or skip the tool. Reserve `--privileged` for genuine needs like Docker-in-Docker.

questions.explanation: --privileged removes most isolation. Grant specific capabilities instead; reserve full privileged for genuine needs.
